scenes.summary: Dante describes the agonizing conflict between two opposing desires that leaves him unable to speak or act.
Beatrice begins her discourse by addressing Dante's first question regarding the movement of souls and the validity of Plato's theories.

scenes.scene_name: The Hierarchy of the First Heaven
scenes.summary: Beatrice explains the celestial hierarchy and how different spirits experience the eternal light differently.

scenes.scene_name: The Use of Anthropomorphism in Scripture
scenes.summary: Beatrice explains that Scripture uses human imagery and physical descriptions to make divine truths accessible to human intellect.
Beatrice refutes the idea that souls return to planetary spheres, noting how this error misled the world into astrology.
Beatrice prepares to address Dante's second, less poisonous concern regarding the justice of divine judgment.

scenes.scene_name: The Distinction Between Will and Force
scenes.summary: Beatrice clarifies that true violence against the will is impossible because a steadfast will cannot be overcome by external force.

scenes.scene_name: The Paradox of Piccarda and Costanza
scenes.summary: Beatrice introduces the difficult case of Piccarda's will, which seems to conflict with the memory of her devotion to Costanza.

scenes.scene_name: The Intersection of Force and Will
scenes.summary: Beatrice explains how fear can cause a person to comply with a force without truly changing their fundamental will.
The narrator describes how Beatrice's words bring peace to Dante's opposing desires.
Dante expresses his profound devotion to Beatrice and reflects on how the intellect seeks and finds satisfaction in truth.
Dante explains that doubt is natural to man and uses it as a bridge to pose a new, difficult question to Beatrice.
Beatrice looks at Dante with eyes full of divine love, causing him to succumb to her overwhelming presence.
